title: Quentin Tarantino on ‘Once Upon a Time … in Hollywood’ ... the Book!
format: Quentin Tarantino sits down with Sean Fennessey and Chris Ryan to talk about novelizing 'Once Upon a Time in Hollywood': the lost history of the genre, a deep dive into James Bridges' filmography, and an obscure rape-revenge movie he can't stop pushing on people.

segments.heading: The lost art of the novelization
segments.note: Tarantino's history lesson before the interview even gets to his own book

films.title: Meatballs
films.note: His go-to example of just how strange the genre could get.

films.title: The Naked Kiss
films.note: One of Sam Fuller's own novelizations, since he wrote it himself.

films.title: The Big Red One
films.note: Fuller's novelization of his own film, which Tarantino says beats the movie.

films.title: Paradise Alley
films.note: Sylvester Stallone wrote this novelization himself.

films.title: The Wind and the Lion
films.note: John Milius's own novelization of his film.

films.title: Close Encounters of the Third Kind
films.note: Spielberg got the novelization credit but didn't write it.

films.title: Star Wars
films.note: George Lucas didn't write this one either; Alan Dean Foster did.

films.title: The Omen
films.note: David Seltzer's novelization outsold horror bestsellers of the day; Tarantino says he ran out of time and the back half just proses the script.

segments.heading: In conversation
segments.note: the 'Once Upon a Time in Hollywood' novelization

films.title: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
films.note: The film the whole book, and this conversation, is built around.

films.title: Reservoir Dogs
films.note: His discarded first novelization idea; he wrote two chapters before switching subjects.

films.title: Rosemary's Baby
films.note: A chapter uses film criticism as narrative to set up Polanski's status at the time.

films.title: The Wrecking Crew
films.note: The real Sharon Tate set visit he didn't plan to write into a chapter until he was already writing it.

films.title: Death Proof
films.note: Marks how long he'd been sitting on unused 'Once Upon a Time' material, since right after this one.

films.title: The Hateful Eight
films.note: Ryan compares the novel's narrator voice to Tarantino's own chapter-break narration in this film.

segments.heading: The James Bridges deep dive
segments.note: how he ended up rewatching an entire filmography

films.title: September 30, 1955
films.note: The novelization he's read three times over his life; he avoided the film itself for decades because the book set the bar too high.

films.title: Colossus: The Forbin Project
films.note: One of Bridges' scripts from before he became a director.

films.title: The Appaloosa
films.note: Another of his early writing credits.

films.title: The Baby Maker
films.note: Bridges' directorial debut, with Barbara Hershey.

films.title: The Paper Chase
films.note: Where his long collaboration with cinematographer Gordon Willis began.

films.title: The China Syndrome
films.note: Bridges' breakout commercial hit.

films.title: Urban Cowboy
films.note: The hit that followed China Syndrome.

films.title: Mike's Murder
films.note: Where Bridges' visual style turns unexpectedly Antonioni-esque.

films.title: Perfect
films.note: The other film where that Antonioni-influenced L.A. imagery shows up.

films.title: Eyewitness
films.note: Tarantino thinks William Hurt is miscast as a janitor in the film; the novelization, told in first person, is better.

films.title: Altered States
films.note: Cited for why Hurt reads as too obviously brilliant to play a nine-to-five janitor.

films.title: Next Stop, Greenwich Village
films.note: Lenny Baker is his model for who should've played the Eyewitness lead instead.

films.title: Zabriskie Point
films.note: The one Antonioni film Tarantino actually likes.

films.title: Summer of '42
films.note: The unacknowledged tonal predecessor to September 30, 1955.

films.title: Such Good Friends
films.note: Cited sarcastically as an Otto Preminger 'masterpiece.'

films.title: Hardly Working
films.note: And Jerry Lewis's 'masterpiece,' same joke.

films.title: The Lady in Red
films.note: The John Sayles-scripted film his fictional actress Trudy Frazier would remake in this universe.

films.title: Agnes of God
films.note: A role Trudy might have played, per the novel's alt-history casting.

films.title: Ordinary People
films.note: Another role name-checked for Trudy in the same bit.

films.title: Mank
films.note: Would've paid to see it in a theater if it hadn't gone straight to Netflix.

films.title: Freaky
films.note: One of only two 2020 releases he'd have paid to see theatrically.

films.title: Unhinged
films.note: The other one, the Russell Crowe road-rage thriller.

films.title: Savaged
films.note: Released in the US as Avenged, an obscure rape-revenge movie he can't stop recommending, plot and all.

films.title: Ms .45
films.note: The genre touchstone that got Avenged recommended to him.

films.title: Despicable Me 2
films.note: The first movie his 15-month-old son watched start to finish, over several sittings.

films.title: The Hindenburg
films.note: Watched on a 35mm IB Technicolor print, paired with Inglourious Basterds for a New Beverly WWII double bill.

films.title: The Drowning Pool
films.note: A 1975 movie draft pick cited as proof of how high that year's floor was.

films.title: Night Moves
films.note: Another 1975 pick; he says it'd be stretched into a six-hour show if made today.

films.title: The Hunter
films.note: Steve McQueen's final film, which plays better imagined as a TV pilot than as a movie.

films.title: Coonskin
films.note: A friend's toddler daughter's favorite movie, to Tarantino's delight.

films.title: Day of the Woman
films.note: Better known as I Spit on Your Grave, the joke answer for what not to show his son first.

films.title: F9
films.note: Cited as evidence that big franchise movies are starting to feel like TV.

films.title: The Sparks Brothers
films.note: Said he'd rather see this than F9.

films.title: Thief
films.note: Robert Prosky's dialogue delivery gets relitigated after a Rewatchables recasting debate.

films.title: Manhunter
films.note: William Petersen's Will Graham, the recasting suggestion that set Tarantino off.

films.title: No Country for Old Men
films.note: Cited for how you can't unsee an actor once you've read them a certain way: Javier Bardem, in this case.

films.title: First Blood
films.note: Tarantino would rather adapt David Morrell's novel than remake this.

films.title: The Fighter
films.note: David O. Russell's line about giving up on chasing the muse and just wanting to make a good movie.

films.title: The Day They Robbed the Bank of England
films.note: Peter O'Toole's first film, co-starring Aldo Ray, shot right before Lawrence of Arabia.

films.title: Lawrence of Arabia
films.note: The role O'Toole made right after Bank of England.

films.title: Grindhouse
films.note: Its poor box office briefly got him blacklisted from studio submissions, then flooded with them.

films.title: Pulp Fiction
films.note: Hypothetical: without this becoming a phenomenon, would he ever have gotten an auteur career at all.

films.title: Kill Bill: Vol. 1
films.note: The abandoned plan for a trilogy plus anime prequel, cited as the kind of expansion he's now sworn off.

films.title: New York, New York
films.note: His model for how he'd recut Once Upon a Time in Hollywood if runtime were no object.

Unproduced: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood stage play
Ads: Netflix FIFA World Cup Launch Edition

Close Encounters of the Third Kind.title: Close Encounters of the Third Kind

Once Upon a Time in Hollywood.title: Once Upon a Time... in Hollywood

The Day They Robbed the Bank of England.title: The Day They Robbed the Bank of England
